title: SEGMENT 3 — Why methylation matters — CpG islands, imprinting, X-inactivation, retrotransposons, cancer

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — Why methylation matters — CpG islands, imprinting, X-inactivation, retrotransposons, cancer
Before we get to the chemistry, I want to make a case for why anyone should care to measure methylation in the first place.
Because the technique is real work — bisulfite is harsh, the libraries are tricky, the analysis has its own quirks — and you should know what you're buying with the effort.
Let me lay out five biological domains where methylation is central, because once you see those, the motivation for genome-wide methylation maps becomes self-evident.
Domain one: CpG island promoters and gene regulation.
I mentioned CpG islands briefly in Segment 1; let me unpack them.
A CpG island is a short stretch of genome — typically 500 to 2,000 base pairs — that is unusually rich in CpG dinucleotides.

Most of the genome is CpG-depleted, because methylated CpGs are mutational hotspots: the methyl group makes the cytosine prone to deamination, converting 5mC into thymine, which permanently mutates the sequence.
Over evolutionary time, methylated CpGs in the bulk genome have been gradually replaced by TpGs through this mutational pressure.

But about 70 percent of human gene promoters sit inside CpG islands, which have somehow escaped this depletion — because in the germline, they are kept unmethylated, and unmethylated CpGs do not deaminate at high rates.
So CpG islands are the protected refugia of CpGs in the genome.
CpG islands at promoters are normally unmethylated, and when they're unmethylated the associated gene is permissive for transcription.
When a CpG island gets aberrantly methylated, the associated gene gets shut off.

This rule — CpG island methylation equals gene silencing — is the most important regulatory consequence of methylation, and it's the one that drives most of the clinical interest in methylation patterns.
A tumor suppressor gene with a methylated promoter is a silenced tumor suppressor.
A cell-identity gene with a methylated promoter is a locked-off cell identity.
The CpG island methylome is, in many ways, the regulatory methylome.
Domain two: X-inactivation.
Every mammalian female cell carries two X chromosomes, but only one is transcriptionally active in each cell.
The other is silenced, in a process called X-inactivation, that happens early in development and is maintained for the rest of the cell's life.
The silenced X is heavily methylated across its CpG island promoters, and that methylation is what locks in the silenced state.

X-inactivation was one of the first systems where the role of DNA methylation in long-term gene silencing was firmly established, and it remains a textbook example of methylation-mediated inheritance of gene expression states.
Domain three: genomic imprinting.
Most genes are expressed from both parental alleles — the copy you inherited from your mother and the copy you inherited from your father — at roughly equal levels.
But a small set of genes, perhaps a few hundred in the human genome, are imprinted: only one parental allele is expressed, while the other is silenced.
The silencing is set up in the germline of the parent of origin and inherited through fertilization.
The mechanism is methylation.
The silenced allele carries a methylated imprinting control region; the expressed allele does not.
Imprinting is biologically critical — disruption of imprinting causes diseases like Prader-Willi and Angelman syndromes, and it's a recurring theme in cancers.
Methylation is the molecular memory that keeps an imprinted gene silenced on the right parental allele.
Domain four: retrotransposon silencing.

Almost half of the human genome consists of repetitive sequences derived from ancient retrotransposons — bits of viral and parasitic DNA that copied themselves around the genome over evolutionary time.

Most of these are inactivated, but if they were ever to wake up and start copying themselves again, the genome would become unstable, and the consequences would include cancer and developmental disorders.
The way the cell keeps them quiet is heavy methylation.
Retrotransposon DNA is among the most heavily methylated DNA in the genome, and that methylation is what physically prevents transcription from initiating in these elements.

In cells where methylation breaks down — for example, after global demethylating drug treatment, or in certain cancers — retrotransposons can reactivate, with downstream consequences for genomic stability.
Domain five: cancer.
This is the big one clinically.
Cancer cells have profoundly disrupted methylation patterns, and the disruption has a characteristic signature.
Global hypomethylation — the bulk genome, especially the repetitive regions, loses methylation, which contributes to genomic instability and retrotransposon reactivation.

Focal hypermethylation at CpG island promoters — specific tumor suppressor genes get their promoters methylated and silenced, which is functionally equivalent to losing those tumor suppressors by mutation, but without any change to the DNA sequence.

The combination — losing methylation where you should have it, gaining it where you shouldn't — is so consistent across cancer types that methylation patterns alone can often classify a tumor's tissue of origin.
This is the basis for methylation-based liquid biopsies, which we'll cover in detail in Segment 15.
There's also methylation in aging — the so-called epigenetic clock, where methylation at a few hundred specific CpG sites changes systematically with chronological age.
There's methylation in environmental exposures — smoking, diet, and stress all leave methylation footprints.

There's methylation in cell-type identity — every cell type in the body has its own characteristic methylation pattern, which can be used to identify the cell of origin of a piece of DNA found in blood plasma.
All of these biological domains drive demand for methylation measurement, and all of them require methods that work across the whole genome at single-base resolution.
So the question is: how do you actually read a methyl group?
The polymerase can't see it.
The four-base genetic code doesn't encode it.
You need a chemical trick to convert the invisible methylation status into a visible sequence difference.
That trick is bisulfite conversion.
